narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared strong aspects of your background, including a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—which stands out above the typical 3.21 average—and middle-family income status, along with planning to attend full-time like most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 65.9%, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors in your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. The biggest push toward the failure prediction came from your LSAT score of 34, which is below the average of 36.7 and signaled to the model a higher bar exam struggle compared to peers with stronger scores. Next, your Year 1 school decile of 6—slightly above the average 5.71—still contributed toward failure because it didn't stand out enough to offset other concerns, placing you mid-pack among typical students. Your middle family income bracket rounded out the top influences, also nudging the prediction toward failure as it aligns with profiles the model associates with added challenges in bar success. While your undergraduate performance and full-time plans offered some counterbalance, these top three factors outweighed them for this specific assessment. Compared to typical students who get approved, your LSAT and early academic decile were notably weaker, even if your overall GPA shines. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model focuses on bar passage patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening early-year grades if possible, or seeking programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. Building bar prep experience through targeted courses could also help reframe your profile for future applications. You're clearly capable, and with focused steps, stronger opportunities await.
